words: Dixie Baugh Groseclose:  Landed down here at Arrow Junction, now Arrow, nine miles
down, you
know, down below.
SAM SCHRAGER:  How many families were there? Living right in that area? I mean, were
there very many? There was more than one Groseclose family, is that
right?

speaker: DG
words: When we came here the Foglejsongs were here then, werent they?

speaker: Edward Groseclose
words: Yeah.

speaker: DG
words: Kent Fogelsong and Will Grosecloses and Crabtee, let's see, the Kinzers.

speaker: DG
words: Is that right?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, Kinzer, Maxwell. Well they was— start in with down there- Foglesong

speaker: EG
words: he was a— there was a little scrap of ground down there about

speaker: EG
words: seventeen or eighteen acres that four or five people homesteaded on it

speaker: EG
words: a while, and Foglesong come out here and bought in on that. Well, Foglesong

speaker: EG
words: come in here in 1906. My oldest brother, Levi, went East and

speaker: EG
words: got married and Foglesong and Crabtree come back with him. And then

speaker: EG
words: bought Crabtree out Jurdin, Jim Jurdin. The place where Crabtree and Jim

speaker: EG
words: Jurdin had is where the museum is there at Arrow now. That was the Jim

speaker: EG
words: Jurdin homestead. They had to carry their water from the Clearwater

speaker: EG
words: River up there. And there was a big family of Jurdins. They water

speaker: EG
words: witched it up there about four or five not very big boys.

speaker: SS
words: Did they get any?

speaker: EG
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: Did they get any when they water wiched it?

speaker: EG
words: They water witched it up by the bucket! (Laughter)

speaker: DG
words: That's his way of jokin'— they water wiched it by hand.

speaker: EG
words: The way they got it up there.

speaker: SS
words: Was your brother, Levi, was he a lawyer?

speaker: EG
words: Well, a little bit, he studied law some and him and- oh, I can't re

speaker: EG
words: call the fellow's name now- at Lewiston, they worked together a while.

speaker: EG
words: He had him an office here at Juliaetta a little while. The main

speaker: EG
words: thing he done was take care of things for this other lawyer. He never

speaker: EG
words: stayed with one thing very long, only farmin'. He stayed with that as

speaker: EG
words: long as he could work.

speaker: EG
words: So, now, most of them people that lived there; were most of them from

speaker: EG
words: Virginia?

speaker: EG
words: Yes, there was a little Virginia down here. Well, you see there was

speaker: EG
words: ten of our family. And then my uncle come in here, Uncle Jim, and he

speaker: EG
words: had a family of nine. And then Maeci's dad up here, well, there was

speaker: EG
words: only six of them. Only six of that family. They located here in Julia

speaker: EG
words: etta. But the Virginians down the canyon there was Fogleson and thenwell,

speaker: EG
words: the Groseeoses homesteaded in there; Will homesteaded in the

speaker: EG
words: Catholic Gulch and then my sister she had the place where Albrightswhere

speaker: EG
words: Lora Jean Albright lives. The most of the place they've got

speaker: EG
words: there was her's. Most of her place was across the river; across the

speaker: EG
words: Potlatch up on that hill. And then my brother, Albert, he had a home

speaker: EG
words: stead where that great, big walnut tree stands up there above the road.

speaker: EG
words: His house was right there by that. Well, that growed by his spring.

speaker: EG
words: Where his homestead was, and then my brother, Marion, had where the

speaker: EG
words: concrete house used to be there. They got a trailer house in there now.

speaker: EG
words: And then, my uncle, Jim, he had the place— well, where all them locust

speaker: EG
words: trees is.

speaker: DG
words: Just the other side of George Johnson-

speaker: EG
words: Just about a quarter^the other side of George Johnson.

speaker: DG
words: Where the water comes down, where the waterfall is.

speaker: EG
words: Them waterfalls there, the water comes down the hill, they was on

speaker: EG
words: his place. He had all them springs along there. And then there was—

speaker: EG
words: well, above Marion's place there was a Steve Kimberling, originally

speaker: EG
words: there was about ten of them, but they was scattered out over the

speaker: EG
words: country. And then Tom Hall, son-in-law of his, had a homestead up the

speaker: EG
words: canyon above Kimberling.

speaker: SS
words: A son-in-law of Kimberlings?

speaker: EG
words: Yes. Son-in-law of Kimberling.

speaker: DG
words: Did they all come from Virginia, too?

speaker: BG
words: Yes, they were all Virginians.

speaker: SS
words: Were they all from more or less the same part of Virginia?

speaker: EG
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Which part was that?

speaker: EG
words: Well, Birch Garden and Ceres. Birch Garden, well it showed on the map.

speaker: EG
words: On the map of Virginia Birch Garden is shown there and Ceres is in

speaker: EG
words: narrow Bland County. Blfcnd County looks like a beaver skin. It's got a tail

speaker: EG
words: runs around in there.

speaker: DG
words: He did have a book on it, but he's got it loaned out.

speaker: EG
words: Any geography show: Virginia and the counties and all that.

speaker: SS
words: Were the Grosecloses the first to relocate out here from that part of

speaker: SS
words: Virginia?

speaker: EG
words: No, the Halls and Kimberlings come here first. The Kimberlings they

speaker: EG
words: come up to Palouse and they went broke in '93, and they come down in

speaker: EG
words: here then. The Kimberlings and Halls come down in here after that.

speaker: EG
words: They come in here really before the railroad.

speaker: SS
words: Now, had they known each other back in Virginia?

speaker: EG
words: Yes.

speaker: SS
words: Were they related— was there relations kin between those two, the

speaker: SS
words: Kimberlings and say the Grosecloses? Before?

speaker: EG
words: My Uncle Jim married a Kimberling. So they was kinda mixed up. We

speaker: EG
words: wasn't related to the Kimberlings, but my uncle's family was. And then

speaker: EG
words: they was two Halls lived up the Little Potlatch Canyon. Old Tom Hall

speaker: EG
words: and Lee had homesteads up the Little Potlatch Canyon. They were Vir

speaker: EG
words: ginians. And then, well, there was another Virginian in there, that

speaker: EG
words: Knowles. Knowles took up the place where Johnson is, but he got funny.

speaker: EG
words: And he was an engraver by trade. And he engraved some United States

speaker: EG
words: currency. And so, he had an indoor job for I don't know how many years

speaker: EG
words: for that. So then, they sold out to Albrights.

speaker: SS
words: He didn't get by with his engraving for very long then?

speaker: EG
words: I don't think very long. I don't know. Then there was another Groses-

speaker: EG
words: Neil, he homesteaded over across the river.

speaker: DG
words: Not a Groseclose.

speaker: EG
words: He was a Virginian, not a Groseclose. Yeah, but he was Virginian.

speaker: SS
words: Neil was his last name?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah, Neil was his last name. Jack Neil.

speaker: SS
words: Jack Neil. He had a family of five, yeah, three boys-

speaker: SS
words: Were the Halls and the Neils, were those families related to the others

speaker: SS
words: at all? To the Grosecloses and the others?

speaker: DG
words: No.

speaker: EG
words: No.

speaker: SS
words: No relation at all.

speaker: SS
words: Neils wasn't directly, but after the Neils children growed up, well

speaker: SS
words: their families married. Then one of the Grosecloses married a grand

speaker: SS
words: daughter of the Neils.

speaker: SS
words: Well, now I'm just curious about what the thinking was. Why the families

speaker: SS
words: wanted to stay close to the people that they'd known back-

speaker: EC
words: No, it wasn't that, it was just that homestead land down in here on

speaker: SS
words: the reservation, and they all come in here and got homesteads.

speaker: DG
words: Just wanted to go West and get rich, I imagine.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah. The Halls and the Kimberlings, they come in here because they

speaker: EG
words: went broke in the Palouse and this was the next place to light that

speaker: EG
words: they could get holt, you know to try to start again, was down in this

speaker: EG
words: here Indian Reservation here.

speaker: SS
words: How many of the families had known each other back in Virginia, do you

speaker: SS
words: think before they came out? Had most of them known the others?

speaker: EG
words: Yes, these all knowed each other.

speaker: DH
words: The Knowles, did you really know them? Not the Knowles, did you?

speaker: EG
words: Knowles, no, we didn't know the Knowles.

speaker: DG
words: The Halls-

speaker: EG
words: The Halls and the Kimberlings, Kinzers-

speaker: DG
words: Foglesongs.

speaker: EG
words: Huh?

speaker: DG
words: I say the Fogelsongs.

speaker: EG
words: Well the Fogelsongs they were related.

speaker: DG
words: Kimberlings and Groscloses and Wilsons.

speaker: SS
words: Did the Wilsons know the Grosecloses back in Virginia?

speaker: EG
words: Yes, my brother, Will and Dave Wilson, they were pals, and they come to

speaker: EG
words: this country and they homesteaded adjoining. And they trapped and hunted

speaker: EG
words: and trapped together. Bought furs if someone sold 'em.

speaker: SS
words: How was that Wilson related to you?

speaker: DG
words: Well, he was my stepfather. Then there was^Baughs in later years came

speaker: DG
words: and in there and they were related to the Wilsons, and they were Vir

speaker: DG
words: ginians.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, they were Virginians; come from the same-

speaker: DG
words: But they come in about 1917.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, and then the Steeles. George Steele come along, too, with his

speaker: EG
words: family.

speaker: DG
words: And that was along about that time, wasn't it?

speaker: EG
words: He come right along about the same time Baughs did.

speaker: DG
words: And they were all Virginians.

speaker: SS
words: What do you think it had been like for the— for your family in Virginia?

speaker: SS
words: I figured that it wasn't so great, or if it was real good they probably

speaker: SS
words: wouldn't have wanted to come out here. They must have figured they

speaker: SS
words: would improve— make a better life out here, I would guess.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, they did. Well—

speaker: DG
words: The younger ones could take up homesteads.

speaker: EG
words: My oldest brother come first, and the reason he got dissatisfied in

speaker: EG
words: Virginia— they was a son-in-law picked a flaw in my grandfather's will.

speaker: EG
words: And he lost quite a bit of land. Well, they had this here will in court

speaker: EG
words: for twenty years before they ever settled it. The grandfather willed

speaker: EG
words: all of the land of the Chestnut Ridge survey to his boys, and gave the

speaker: EG
words: girls a thousand dolalrs apiece more than the boys boys the

speaker: EG
words: Chestnut Ridge survey. Well he paid ten cents an acre for this land in

speaker: EG
words: the hills there. At that time you know, well, Batrick Henry signed the

speaker: EG
words: deed to it. So, it wasn't a giving 'em so very much, but I don't know

speaker: EG
words: exactly how many— twenty, thirty thousand acres— I don't know what

speaker: EG
words: it was. Anyway, when the courts— there wasn't a man in the country

speaker: EG
words: that knew what the Chestnut Ridge survey was. Well, this here son-inlaw

speaker: EG
words: said that the Chestnut Ridge survey just included the land that

speaker: EG
words: was on the Chestnut Ridge, and the other claimed that—

speaker: DG
words: Just like a bunch of land going down the Potlatch Ridge, you know.

speaker: EG
words: it included all the— it was Chestnut Ridge survey, you know,— well

speaker: EG
words: I guess you might say like the Nez Perce survey here. Over there at

speaker: EG
words: Nez Perce, the Nez Perce survey takes in the whole Nez Perce Indian

speaker: EG
words: Reservation. But if somebody didn't know all that and some fellow'd

speaker: EG
words: say, "Well, all that Nez Perce country is up,around Nez Perce." That

speaker: EG
words: was the way I figured the way it musta been, that they give the name of

speaker: EG
words: a survey that surveyed all that country out; called it a Chestnut Ridge

speaker: EG
words: Survey. But there was nobody knew for sure what it was. So the court

speaker: EG
words: finally divided it up equal; the land up equal between the girls and

speaker: EG
words: the boys. So we lost some 400 acres in there.

speaker: SS
words: You say, "we" you mean—?

speaker: EG
words: My family, yeah. My direct family lost that. So my brother, Will,

speaker: EG
words: says, I'm agoing' to Idaho. The geography tells about that country

speaker: EG
words: there in the West; homestead land. And it's warmed by the Chinook

speaker: EG
words: winds from the west there, that Japanese current and it's a mild clim

speaker: EG
words: ate in there1,1 he says, "I'm going out and look at that." Dad says,

speaker: EG
words: "You go look at it and if you like it I'll follow you." So that's why

speaker: EG
words: we come.

speaker: SS
words: You know this survey— I'm thinking about that— this was all land that

speaker: SS
words: had been given to your grandfather by the governor, or something like

speaker: SS
words: that?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah. Patrick Henry, he was the governor of Virginia there-

speaker: DG
words: Was it give to 'em, or did you say they give

speaker: EG
words: He bought it for ten cents an acre.

speaker: DG
words: Bought it from the government, huh?

speaker: EG
words: Yeah. That wasn't very much. Well, this whole Nez Perce— or a good

speaker: EG
words: percent of this country in here, twenty-five hundred square miles, of

speaker: EG
words: this country right in here was bought from the Nez Perce Indians for

speaker: EG
words: twenty-five cents an acre. And then they got into war and^never paid

speaker: EG
words: the Indians for it, til^maVYj years after. After the Indians got

speaker: EG
words: educated enough to know what they was doin', then they sued the govern

speaker: EG
words: ment for this two bits and the interest on it. So the Indians got

speaker: EG
words: quite a lot of money out of it. The interest amounted to a whole lot

speaker: EG
words: more than the principal when they paid for it.

speaker: SS
words: You know, what I've read about— the little bit that I've read, I don't

speaker: SS
words: know much about Virginia and that part of the country, was that families

speaker: SS
words: would stay there for generations and generations, it would be the same

speaker: SS
words: families in one place, and had a lot of good strong roots there.

speaker: EG
words: My great grandfather come into that country there about 1780, when he

speaker: EG
words: come into that country there. And my great grandfather— they had the

speaker: EG
words: book of Blftnd County tells about him, that's Henry; Big Henry. He

speaker: EG
words: was a building a house— his father was having him a house built for

speaker: EG
words: him to get married in and they was a building it outta logs, and there

speaker: EG
words: was one log that they decided they'd have to change e^s with to make

speaker: EG
words: the building straight, and it was quite a log, and so the carpenters

speaker: EG
words: was arguing over that, they didn't want to change that big log. So—

speaker: EG
words: there's two stories to this— some says that they put it up therethat's

speaker: EG
words: the story my dad had that they'd laid the log up, but they

speaker: EG
words: was a arguing about changing it, and he says, "Lay the log on my shoul

speaker: EG
words: der and I'll change it." They thought they'd measure from the groundbut

speaker: EG
words: they laid that thirty-two foot oak log on his shoulder and he turn

speaker: EG
words: ed it around and put it back up on there.

speaker: EG
words: Now that's the story my dad tells, but the book has it, that he picked this log up

speaker: EG
words: offa the ground

speaker: EG
words: and laid it up there. Well, to pick a log up that long and lay it up

speaker: EG
words: there all at onct, is quite a thing, but to pick up one end and laid

speaker: EG
words: it over and pushed it over and then went and got the other^and laid it

speaker: EG
words: up there where it cross balanced, that wouldn't abeen lifting more than

speaker: EG
words: half the log at a time. But then, the way it tells it, he picked it

speaker: EG
words: all up. So, I don't know, it musta been somewhere around 1500 pounds,

speaker: EG
words: or something like that if he picked it up. He was— well they claimed

speaker: EG
words: he could take a thirty gallon keg of whiskey, pick it up this way, and

speaker: EG
words: take him a drink out of itJ! Then he lived in that house there and

speaker: EG
words: raised his family and then.one boy, William, Dr. William, that was my

speaker: EG
words: grandfather, he lived in that house, and then his youngest son, Dal,

speaker: EG
words: lived there. And when we sold out, my dad sold out, he told to Dal -

speaker: EG
words: so well, they divided the home place among the three boys; Cass and

speaker: EG
words: Jim and Dal. So Jim come out to this country; he sold out to Dal.

speaker: SS
words: How were they related to you?

speaker: EG
words: That was my dad's—

speaker: SS
words: Jim was your dad?

speaker: EG
words: Jim was my dad, yeah. Big Jim. And the other, Little Jim, he come

speaker: EG
words: out here, he was a small fella. Now Maeci Nye, up here now she had

speaker: EG
words: Old Uncle Jim, and Uncle Jimmy, that's the way she described them, but

speaker: EG
words: the people of the country, speaking about 'em, they called 'em Big Jim

speaker: EG
words: and Little Jim.

speaker: SS
words: Who was Little Jim?

speaker: EG
words: Little Ji? he was my mother's brother, he — Little Jim was the grandfather

speaker: EG
words: of this fella that runs the service station.

speaker: DG
words: His mother was a Groseclose and married a Groseclose.

speaker: SS
words: Well, was Maeci's grandfather some kind of bigwig back there? I thought

speaker: SS
words: she had said that her mother's father had-

speaker: EG
words: Her mother someway was related to Henry Clay, through somewhere

speaker: EG
words: through there. I don't know just how close they was. She was a Tiller

speaker: EG
words: and where the Tillers and the Clays come from I don t know.

speaker: SS
words: I thought her mother's father—^at least I've heard about that— when

speaker: SS
words: you'd stay - like the families being in one place for so long like that,

speaker: SS
words: that when they'd get a fmid going or something like that it' go on

speaker: SS
words: for years and years.

speaker: EG
words: Well, they never happened to have a feud in that country there, not

speaker: EG
words: right in there. The only thing, you know, was a— they split off,

speaker: EG
words: they were Southerners and during the war my father and two brothers

speaker: EG
words: and a bond boy— Now back there in them old days, you could take poor

speaker: EG
words: children, and my grandfather had a boy and a girl, and they had to

speaker: EG
words: keep 'em til they was twenty- one years old and they give the boy a

speaker: EG
words: suit of clothes and a horse and, I don't know, they give the girls

speaker: EG
words: something in a bed or something that way, that they give her when she

speaker: EG
words: was come of age. I don't know what become of the girl but the boy

speaker: EG
words: went to the army, and they never heard tell of him again. Now, I've

speaker: EG
words: always thought about that, he went into the army afightin' for to keep

speaker: EG
words: the slaves and he was practically as much of a slave as what the others

speaker: EG
words: was. Well, you know what people will ... And such funny things as

speaker: EG
words: they can teach people, and such foolish things and then people will

speaker: EG
words: fall for it. Like our churches splittin'. They split over Noah of

speaker: EG
words: making a curse to his son. Noah, you know, got drunk and undressed

speaker: EG
words: himself in a tent and one son went in and seen him, well, he didn t

speaker: EG
words: think anything serious about it, but he comes out and told the other

speaker: EG
words: boys, laughed about it, well, they thought that was terrible; a man

speaker: EG
words: so naked, so they got a blanket and went backwards and covered him up.

speaker: EG
words: And ,when he was gettin' over the drunk, they told him about it and he

speaker: EG
words: said, "Cursed was this I believe was his name- A servant of

speaker: EG
words: servants should his seed be all the days of their lives." Well, then,

speaker: EG
words: they say that his seed— his people- went into Africa and then turned

speaker: EG
words: black and that made it legal for to make slaves out of the black people

speaker: EG
words: So they was ten million niggers brought into this country and sold as

speaker: EG
words: slaves. And then they got into that big war over it. You know, the

speaker: EG
words: funny thing, we talk about— tell now about all our booze and tobacco

speaker: EG
words: being such terrible things, well in the long run, this Civil War was

speaker: EG
words: caused way back there when Old Noah got drunk and pronounced the curse

speaker: EG
words: on his son. His son was supposed to produce the black men and they

speaker: EG
words: come over here as slaves and this country got in a war over it. And

speaker: EG
words: had that terrible war over that, and that was all caused by booze.

speaker: SS
words: All because of the booze?

speaker: EG
words: Huh?

speaker: SS
words: Because of the booze.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, because Old Noah got drunk.

speaker: SS
words: What did you think about slavery?

speaker: EG
words: Oh, it was a dirty thing. It was terrible.

speaker: DG
words: His grandfather owned slaves.

speaker: EG
words: My grandfather owned slaves, yes. And he was out in the country where

speaker: EG
words: they didn't grow cotton though. He didn't have any use for 'em, I don't

speaker: EG
words: know, just a few, two or three or four. I don't know. Don't know how

speaker: EG
words: many he had, just a very few. But, he leaned in that direction, so

speaker: EG
words: that his boys went into the Southern Army. My Uncle Cass was a cap

speaker: EG
words: tain, and my dad was a first lieutenant in the Southern Army.

speaker: SS
words: During the Civil War?

speaker: EG
words: During the Civil War, Yeah. They was only in actual duty about two years

speaker: EG
words: til they got captured. And then they was in prison for two years.

speaker: SS
words: That must have been pretty rough, being in prison.

speaker: EG
words: Well, I guess the North didn't treat 'em so bad. 'Course, they had to

speaker: EG
words: stay in there. I heard him tell about one time while he was in prison,

speaker: EG
words: there was a guard awalkin' along and he asked the guard if he would

speaker: EG
words: break him off some kind of a bush that was growin' out there, great

speaker: EG
words: long ? , if he would get him one of them for a fishin' pole.

speaker: EG
words: "Yes," he says, "I'll get you one." And then there was a place over

speaker: EG
words: somewhere where they could get stuff, and this here guard^he works

speaker: EG
words: his way out, you know, and got this and brung it over and put it

speaker: EG
words: through to him and say, he says, "Will you go over there to a place

speaker: EG
words: somewhere across there and get me some g^er candy?" So, he went over

speaker: EG
words: and brought the guard some candy, and brought it back give it to him.

speaker: EG
words: Yeah, oh, they had— if they acted sensible they could get along good

speaker: EG
words: together.

speaker: SS
words: Did your father— do you think he bore a lot of anger about the Civil

speaker: SS
words: War afterwards?

speaker: EG
words: No, he wasn't too hostile. A lot of people did own to that, but they

speaker: EG
words: all had that foolish idea of, well- they could hire a nigger or a nig

speaker: EG
words: ger come and work for 'em, or he could cook for 'em— a negro cook he

speaker: EG
words: could have work on the farm but when it come to the table to eat, why,

speaker: EG
words: he never eat it. Didn't make any difference how much room there was

speaker: EG
words: on the table, a black man didn't eat at the same table with a white.

speaker: DG
words: If you have that shut off, I was gonna find some of this things a

speaker: DG
words: little bit on the order of what he writes. He's wrote about the neg

speaker: DG
words: roes and so on, but I don't know where they're at." My dad told me

speaker: DG
words: with a leary eye, that I would go to hell if I told a lie. Then with

speaker: DG
words: out a pause, he told about Santa Claus." (Apparently this is a quote

speaker: DG
words: which I did not recognize and write as such. F.R.) A name and to

speaker: DG
words: this country a lot of people came. And, of course, they had to name

speaker: DG
words: something, and they called — and some called it Chile, some parts

speaker: DG
words: then Brazil and Argentina, and all the others^n between, the people

speaker: DG
words: they called Indians, the nicest people I have ever seen, the white man

speaker: DG
words: treated them very mean. The white man pushed the Indians from here to

speaker: DG
words: there and so doing lost a lot of hair. What God will do to the white

speaker: DG
words: man treating other men so mean, we have seen a lot and a lot we haven t

speaker: DG
